is one of nine official [regions of England](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England") at the first level of [ITL](/wiki/ITL_(UK) "ITL (UK)") for [statistical](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") purposes.[[a]](#cite_note-5) It consists of the ceremonial counties of [Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire"), [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire"), [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire"), [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") and [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk").[[5]](#cite_note-6) The northern part of the region, consisting of Norfolk, Suffolk and Cambridgeshire, is known as [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia"). The latter region has been considered an informal region in its own right due to its differing cultural identity.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

The **East of England** is one of nine official [regions of England](/wiki/Regions_of_England "Regions of England") at the first level of [ITL](/wiki/ITL_(UK) "ITL (UK)") for [statistical](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") purposes.[[a]](#cite_note-5) It consists of the ceremonial counties of [Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire"), [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/Cambridgeshire "Cambridgeshire"), [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire"), [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") and [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk").[[5]](#cite_note-6) The northern part of the region, consisting of Norfolk, Suffolk and Cambridgeshire, is known as [East Anglia](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia"). The latter region has been considered an informal region in its own right due to its differing cultural identity.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

The population of the East of England in 2024 was 6,576,306.[[3]](#cite_note-popstats-3) [Bedford](/wiki/Bedford "Bedford"), [Luton](/wiki/Luton "Luton"), [Basildon](/wiki/Basildon "Basildon"), [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough "Peterborough"), [Southend-on-Sea](/wiki/Southend-on-Sea "Southend-on-Sea"), [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich"), [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich "Ipswich"), [Colchester](/wiki/Colchester "Colchester"), [Chelmsford](/wiki/Chelmsford "Chelmsford") and [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge") are the most populous settlements.[[7]](#cite_note-8) Peterborough is the largest city in the East of England at 215,000. The southern part of the region lies in the [London commuter belt](/wiki/London_metropolitan_area "London metropolitan area").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/London%2C_Great_Britain_Population_Density_and_Low_Elevation_Coastal_Zones_%285457306673%29.jpg/250px-London%2C_Great_Britain_Population_Density_and_Low_Elevation_Coastal_Zones_%285457306673%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:London,_Great_Britain_Population_Density_and_Low_Elevation_Coastal_Zones_(5457306673).jpg)

England population density and low elevation coastal zones. East of England is particularly vulnerable to [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise").

The East of England has the lowest elevation range in the UK. Twenty per cent of the region is below mean sea level, most of this in North Cambridgeshire, Norfolk and on the Essex Coast.[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10) Most of the remaining area is of low elevation, with extensive glacial deposits.[[10]](#cite_note-11) [The Fens](/wiki/The_Fens "The Fens"), a large area of reclaimed marshland, are mostly in North Cambridgeshire.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The Fens include the lowest point in the country in the village of [Holme](/wiki/Holme,_Cambridgeshire "Holme, Cambridgeshire"): 2.75 metres (9.0 ft) below mean sea level. This area formerly included the body of open water known as [Whittlesey Mere](/wiki/Whittlesey_Mere "Whittlesey Mere"). The highest point in the region is at Clipper Down at 817 ft (249 m) above mean sea level, in the far southwestern corner of the region in the [Ivinghoe Hills](/wiki/Ivinghoe_Hills "Ivinghoe Hills").[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Communities known as [New Towns](/wiki/New_towns_movement "New towns movement"), responses to urban congestion and World War II destruction, appeared in [Basildon](/wiki/Basildon "Basildon") and [Harlow](/wiki/Harlow "Harlow") (Essex), as well as in [Stevenage](/wiki/Stevenage "Stevenage") and [Hemel Hempstead](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead "Hemel Hempstead") (Hertfordshire), in the 1950s and 1960s.[[15]](#cite_note-16) In the late 1960s, the [Roskill Commission](/wiki/Roskill_Commission "Roskill Commission") considered Cublington in Buckinghamshire, [Thurleigh](/wiki/Thurleigh "Thurleigh") in Bedfordshire, [Nuthampstead](/wiki/Nuthampstead "Nuthampstead") in Hertfordshire and [Foulness](/wiki/Foulness "Foulness") in Essex as locations for a possible third airport for London. A new airport was not built, but a former [Royal Air Force base at Stansted](/wiki/RAF_Stansted_Mountfitchet "RAF Stansted Mountfitchet"), which had previously been converted to civilian use redeveloped and expanded in the following decades.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

### Historical use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Historical use")]

The East of England succeeded the [standard statistical region](/wiki/Standard_statistical_regions "Standard statistical regions") East Anglia (which excluded [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire") and [Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire"), then in the [South East](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England")). The East of England [civil defence region](/wiki/Historical_and_alternative_regions_of_England#Civil_defence_regions "Historical and alternative regions of England") was identical to today's region.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

### East Anglia with Home Counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: East Anglia with Home Counties")]

Essex, despite meaning East-Saxons, previously formed part of the [South East England](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England") administrative region, along with Bedfordshire and Hertfordshire, a mixture of definite and debatable [Home counties](/wiki/Home_counties "Home counties"). The earliest use of the term is from 1695. [Charles Davenant](/wiki/Charles_Davenant "Charles Davenant"), in *An essay upon ways and means of supplying the war*, wrote, "The Eleven Home Counties, which are thought in Land Taxes to pay more than their proportion..." then cited a list including these four. The term does not appear to have been used in taxation since the 18th century.[[18]](#cite_note-Davenant-19)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

East Anglia is one of the driest parts of the United Kingdom, with average rainfall ranging from 450 to 750 mm (18 to 30 in).[[19]](#cite_note-20) The area receives such low rainfall amounts because [low pressure](/wiki/Low-pressure_area "Low-pressure area") systems and [weather fronts](/wiki/Weather_front "Weather front") from the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") lose a lot of moisture over land (and therefore are usually much weaker) by the time they reach Eastern England.[[20]](#cite_note-tandfonline.com-21)

Winter (mid-November – mid-March) is mostly cool, but non-prevailing cold easterly winds can affect the area from the continent. These can bring heavy snowfall if the winds interact with a [low-pressure system](/wiki/Low-pressure_area "Low-pressure area") over the Atlantic or [France](/wiki/France "France").[[20]](#cite_note-tandfonline.com-21) Northerly winds also can be cold but are not usually as cold as easterly winds. Westerly winds bring milder and, typically, wetter weather. Southerly winds usually bring mild air (if from the Atlantic or [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa")) but chill if coming from further east than Spain.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

Spring (mid-March – May) is a transitional season that initially can be chilly but is usually warm by late-April/May. The weather at this time is often changeable (within each day) and occasionally showery.[[22]](#cite_note-linkinghub.elsevier.com-23)

Summer (June – mid-September) is usually warm. Continental air from mainland Europe or the [Azores High](/wiki/Azores_High "Azores High") usually leads to at least a few weeks of hot, balmy weather with prolonged warm to hot temperatures. The number of summer storms from the Atlantic, such as the remnants of a [tropical storm](/wiki/Tropical_storm "Tropical storm"), usually coincides with the location of the [jet stream](/wiki/Jet_stream "Jet stream"). The East tends to receive much less rain than the other regions.[[22]](#cite_note-linkinghub.elsevier.com-23)

Autumn (mid-September – mid-November) is usually mild with some days being very unsettled and rainy and others warm. At least part of September and early October in the East have warm and settled weather, but only in rare years is there an [Indian summer](/wiki/Indian_summer "Indian summer") where fine weather marks the entire traditional harvest season.[[22]](#cite_note-linkinghub.elsevier.com-23)

Dust devils were reported in Essex and Cambridgeshire on 17 August 2024, causing minor injuries and some disruption. These small whirlwinds, which form from the ground up, are less powerful than tornadoes. In Essex, they caused tents and gazebos to be lifted during a local event, resulting in minor injuries. Witnesses described the event as unexpected, noting that such phenomena are rare in the area.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

### Elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Elections")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/United_Kingdom_General_Election_2017_Results_Map_%28East_of_England%29.svg/250px-United_Kingdom_General_Election_2017_Results_Map_%28East_of_England%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:United_Kingdom_General_Election_2017_Results_Map_(East_of_England).svg)

General Election results in 2017

In the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election") there was an overall swing of 0.25% from the Conservatives to Labour and the Liberal Democrats lost 16% of its vote. All of Hertfordshire and Suffolk became Conservative. The region's electorate voted 49% Conservative, 22% Labour, 16% UKIP, 8% Liberal Democrat and 4% Green. Like other regions, the division of seats favours the dominant party in the region and the Conservatives had 52, Labour 4 ([Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Cambridge (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Luton South](/wiki/Luton_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Luton South (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Luton North](/wiki/Luton_North_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Luton North (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Norwich South](/wiki/Norwich_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Norwich South (UK Parliament constituency)")), UKIP 1 ([Clacton](/wiki/Clacton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Clacton (UK Parliament constituency)")) and 1 Liberal Democrat ([North Norfolk](/wiki/North_Norfolk_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Norfolk (UK Parliament constituency)")).[[31]](#cite_note-32)

In the [2019 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election"), the Conservatives gained [Peterborough](/wiki/Peterborough_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Peterborough (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Ipswich (UK Parliament constituency)") from Labour. They also gained [North Norfolk](/wiki/North_Norfolk_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Norfolk (UK Parliament constituency)") from the [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") but lost [St Albans](/wiki/St_Albans_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "St Albans (UK Parliament constituency)") to [Daisy Cooper](/wiki/Daisy_Cooper "Daisy Cooper").[[32]](#cite_note-33)

### Civil War and the Protectorate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Civil War and the Protectorate")]

The East of England was a major force and resource for Parliament and, in particular, in the form of the [Eastern Association](/wiki/Eastern_Association "Eastern Association"). [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") came from Huntingdon.[[34]](#cite_note-Macleod_647–665-35)

### Second World War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Second World War")]

Norfolk, Suffolk and Essex played host to the American [VIII Bomber Command](/wiki/Eighth_Air_Force "Eighth Air Force") and [Ninth Air Force](/wiki/Ninth_Air_Force "Ninth Air Force"). The [Imperial War Museum at Duxford](/wiki/Imperial_War_Museum_Duxford "Imperial War Museum Duxford") has an exhibition, commemorating their participation and sacrifice, near to the M11 south of Cambridge.[[34]](#cite_note-Macleod_647–665-35)

Stansted Airport was [RAF Stansted Mountfitchet](/wiki/RAF_Stansted_Mountfitchet "RAF Stansted Mountfitchet"), home to the [344th Bombardment Group](/wiki/126th_Air_Refueling_Wing "126th Air Refueling Wing"). The [de Havilland Mosquito](/wiki/De_Havilland_Mosquito "De Havilland Mosquito") was mainly assembled at Hatfield and Leavesden, although much of the innovative wooden structure originated outside the region from the furniture industry of [High Wycombe](/wiki/High_Wycombe "High Wycombe"); the Mosquito entered service in 1942 with [105 Sqn](/wiki/No._105_Squadron_RAF "No. 105 Squadron RAF") at [RAF Horsham St Faith](/wiki/RAF_Horsham_St_Faith "RAF Horsham St Faith"). [RAF Tempsford](/wiki/RAF_Tempsford "RAF Tempsford") in Bedford is the airfield from where [SOE](/wiki/Special_Operations_Executive "Special Operations Executive") secret agents for Europe took off, with [138 Sqn](/wiki/No._138_Squadron_RAF "No. 138 Squadron RAF") which parachuted agents and equipment and [161 Sqn](/wiki/No._161_Squadron_RAF "No. 161 Squadron RAF") which landed and retrieved agents. [19 Sqn](/wiki/No._19_Squadron_RAF "No. 19 Squadron RAF") at Duxford was the first to be equipped with the Spitfire on 4 August 1938.[[34]](#cite_note-Macleod_647–665-35)

### Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Cold War")]

See also: [United States Air Forces in Europe – Air Forces Africa](/wiki/United_States_Air_Forces_in_Europe_%E2%80%93_Air_Forces_Africa "United States Air Forces in Europe – Air Forces Africa") and [United States Air Force in the United Kingdom](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force_in_the_United_Kingdom "United States Air Force in the United Kingdom")

The [81st Tactical Fighter Wing](/wiki/81st_Training_Wing "81st Training Wing") was at [RAF Bentwaters](/wiki/RAF_Bentwaters "RAF Bentwaters") from January 1952 and also at [RAF Woodbridge](/wiki/RAF_Woodbridge "RAF Woodbridge"); in the late 1980s some of the aircraft went to [RAF Alconbury](/wiki/RAF_Alconbury "RAF Alconbury"). Alconbury closed in 1992 and Bentwaters closed in 1993, with the American air forces being in the area for 42 years; the USAF aircraft subsequently moved to [Spangdahlem Air Base](/wiki/Spangdahlem_Air_Base "Spangdahlem Air Base") in [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate"), Germany.[[35]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-36)

At [RAF Marham](/wiki/RAF_Marham "RAF Marham") in west Norfolk, [214 Sqn](/wiki/No._214_Squadron_RAF "No. 214 Squadron RAF") with the [Vickers Valiant](/wiki/Vickers_Valiant "Vickers Valiant") developed the RAF's refuelling system; later the squadron would be equipped with the [Handley Page Victor](/wiki/Handley_Page_Victor "Handley Page Victor"). Work on refuelling had also taken place at [RAF Tarrant Rushton](/wiki/RAF_Tarrant_Rushton "RAF Tarrant Rushton") in [Dorset](/wiki/Dorset "Dorset").[[35]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-36)

From the 1950s, [RAF Wyton](/wiki/RAF_Wyton "RAF Wyton") was an important reconnaissance base for the RAF, mainly [543 Sqn](/wiki/No._543_Squadron_RAF "No. 543 Squadron RAF"). The base is now home of the [Defence Intelligence Fusion Centre](/wiki/Defence_Intelligence_Fusion_Centre "Defence Intelligence Fusion Centre"), previously known as JARIC, or the Joint Air Reconnaissance Intelligence Centre from 1956.[[35]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Samuel_Whitbread_%281720%E2%80%931796%29_%28copy_after_Joshua_Reynolds%29.jpg/250px-Samuel_Whitbread_%281720%E2%80%931796%29_%28copy_after_Joshua_Reynolds%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samuel_Whitbread_(1720%E2%80%931796)_(copy_after_Joshua_Reynolds).jpg)

[Moto Hospitality](/wiki/Moto_Hospitality "Moto Hospitality") has its headquarters at [Toddington](/wiki/Toddington,_Bedfordshire "Toddington, Bedfordshire") in Bedfordshire (at the [Toddington services](/wiki/Toddington_services "Toddington services")).

[Vauxhall](/wiki/Vauxhall_Motors "Vauxhall Motors") produced its last [Vectra](/wiki/Opel_Vectra "Opel Vectra") in March 2002 at its [Luton plant](/wiki/Vauxhall_Luton "Vauxhall Luton") near the A6/A505 roundabout. ### East Anglia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Flag_of_East_Anglia.svg/250px-Flag_of_East_Anglia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_East_Anglia.svg)

Flag of East Anglia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Great_Witchingham_Hall_-_geograph.org.uk_-_73615.jpg/250px-Great_Witchingham_Hall_-_geograph.org.uk_-_73615.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_Witchingham_Hall_-_geograph.org.uk_-_73615.jpg)

[Great Witchingham Hall](/wiki/Great_Witchingham_Hall "Great Witchingham Hall"), the headquarters of [Bernard Matthews Farms](/wiki/Bernard_Matthews_Farms "Bernard Matthews Farms"), north-west of Norwich at [Great Witchingham](/wiki/Great_Witchingham "Great Witchingham") on the [A1067](/wiki/A1067_road "A1067 road")

The economy in Norfolk, Cambridgeshire and Suffolk is traditionally mostly agricultural. Norfolk is the UK's biggest producer of potatoes. Nationally known companies include the [RAC](/wiki/RAC_Limited "RAC Limited"), [Archant](/wiki/Archant "Archant") (publishing), [Virgin Money](/wiki/Virgin_Money_UK "Virgin Money UK") and [Aviva](/wiki/Aviva "Aviva") (formerly [Norwich Union](/wiki/Norwich_Union "Norwich Union")) in Norwich. In Carrow, to the east of the city, [Colman's](/wiki/Colman%27s "Colman's") makes a wide range of mustards, and [Britvic](/wiki/Britvic "Britvic") makes [Robinsons](/wiki/Robinsons_(drink) "Robinsons (drink)") squash, which was owned by Colman's until 1995. Across the [River Yare](/wiki/River_Yare "River Yare") near the A47/[A146](/wiki/A146_road "A146 road") junction in [Trowse with Newton](/wiki/Trowse "Trowse") is [May Gurney](/wiki/May_Gurney "May Gurney"), the construction company. [Bernard Matthews Farms](/wiki/Bernard_Matthews_Farms "Bernard Matthews Farms") has a large turkey farm on the former [RAF Attlebridge](/wiki/RAF_Attlebridge "RAF Attlebridge") in [Weston Longville](/wiki/Weston_Longville "Weston Longville"). [Campbell Soup](/wiki/Campbell_Soup_Company "Campbell Soup Company") was made in [Kings Lynn](/wiki/Kings_Lynn "Kings Lynn") until 2008, and on the Hardwick Industrial Estate at the A47/[A149](/wiki/A149_road "A149 road") junction is [PinguinLutosa](/w/index.php?title=PinguinLutosa&action=edit&redlink=1 "PinguinLutosa (page does not exist)") the UK, which packs [frozen vegetables](/wiki/Frozen_vegetables "Frozen vegetables"), and [Caithness Crystal](/w/index.php?title=Caithness_Crystal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Caithness Crystal (page does not exist)").[[44]](#cite_note-45)

[Foster Refrigerator](/w/index.php?title=Foster_Refrigerator&action=edit&redlink=1 "Foster Refrigerator (page does not exist)") is the UK's leading manufacturer of commercial refrigerators and [blast chillers](/wiki/Blast_chilling "Blast chilling"), owned by [Illinois Tool Works](/wiki/Illinois_Tool_Works "Illinois Tool Works"), based on the industrial estate; with [Multitone Electronics](/wiki/Multitone_Electronics "Multitone Electronics"), which has a manufacturing plant there, and which invented the [pager](/wiki/Pager "Pager") in 1956, for [St Thomas' Hospital](/wiki/St_Thomas%27_Hospital "St Thomas' Hospital"); and [Snap-on Diagnostics](/wiki/Snap-on "Snap-on") makes [diagnostic tools](/wiki/Machine_fault_diagnosis "Machine fault diagnosis") for garages. British Sugar's [Wissington](/wiki/Wissington,_Norfolk "Wissington, Norfolk") is the world's largest [sugar beet](/wiki/Sugar_beet "Sugar beet") factory in [Methwold](/wiki/Methwold "Methwold"), on the B1160 near the [River Wissey](/wiki/River_Wissey "River Wissey"). [Lotus Cars](/wiki/Lotus_Cars "Lotus Cars") and [Team Lotus](/wiki/Team_Lotus "Team Lotus") are on the eastern edge of the former [RAF Hethel](/wiki/RAF_Hethel "RAF Hethel"), east of [Wymondham](/wiki/Wymondham "Wymondham") (A11) at [Hethel](/wiki/Hethel "Hethel") ([Bracon Ash](/wiki/Bracon_Ash "Bracon Ash")). [Jeyes Group](/w/index.php?title=Jeyes_Group&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jeyes Group (page does not exist)") makes household chemicals in [Thetford](/wiki/Thetford "Thetford"), off the [A134](/wiki/A134_road "A134 road"); [Multiyork](/wiki/Multiyork "Multiyork") makes furniture and [Baxter Healthcare](/wiki/Baxter_International "Baxter International") has a manufacturing plant in the south of the town. [Aunt Bessie](/wiki/Aunt_Bessie "Aunt Bessie") vegetable products (roast potatoes) are made by [Heinz](/wiki/Heinz "Heinz") at [Westwick](/wiki/Westwick,_Norfolk "Westwick, Norfolk"), in a factory built by [Ross Group](/wiki/Ross_Group "Ross Group").[[45]](#cite_note-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/P60ARM_GC_01.jpg/250px-P60ARM_GC_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:P60ARM_GC_01.jpg)

[ARM CPU](/wiki/ARM_architecture "ARM architecture") designed in Cambridge

Around Cambridge on numerous [science parks](/wiki/Science_park "Science park"), are high technology (electronics and biochemistry) companies, such as [ARM Holdings](/wiki/ARM_Holdings "ARM Holdings") on [Peterhouse Technology Park](/w/index.php?title=Peterhouse_Technology_Park&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peterhouse Technology Park (page does not exist)") in the south-east of the town, [Adder Technology](/wiki/Adder_Technology "Adder Technology") ([KVM switches](/wiki/KVM_switch "KVM switch")) at [Bar Hill](/wiki/Bar_Hill "Bar Hill") at the [A14](/wiki/A14_road_(England) "A14 road (England)")/B1050 junction north of the town, [Monsanto](/wiki/Monsanto "Monsanto"), [Play.com](/wiki/Play.com "Play.com") on the Cambridge Business Centre. The [Wellcome Trust Genome Campus](/wiki/Wellcome_Trust_Genome_Campus "Wellcome Trust Genome Campus") has the [European Bioinformatics Institute](/wiki/European_Bioinformatics_Institute "European Bioinformatics Institute") at [Hinxton](/wiki/Hinxton "Hinxton") east of Duxford near the M11 spur for the A11. These form the so-called [Silicon Fen](/wiki/Silicon_Fen "Silicon Fen"). [Marshall Aerospace](/wiki/Marshall_Aerospace "Marshall Aerospace") is at [Cambridge Airport](/wiki/Cambridge_Airport "Cambridge Airport") on the [A1303](/wiki/A1303_road "A1303 road") in the east of the town, towards [Teversham](/wiki/Teversham "Teversham"). South of the airport, [Carl Zeiss NTS](/wiki/Carl_Zeiss_AG "Carl Zeiss AG") makes [scanning electron microscopes](/wiki/Scanning_electron_microscope "Scanning electron microscope") in Cherry Hinton. [Syngenta](/wiki/Syngenta "Syngenta") is to the east of Cambridge, on Capital Park at [Fulbourn](/wiki/Fulbourn "Fulbourn"). [Premier Foods](/wiki/Premier_Foods "Premier Foods") has a large plant in [Histon](/wiki/Histon "Histon") making [Robertson's](/wiki/Robertson%27s "Robertson's") and [Hartley's](/wiki/Hartley%27s "Hartley's") jam, [Gale's](/wiki/Gale%27s "Gale's") honey, [Smash](/wiki/Smash_(instant_mashed_potato) "Smash (instant mashed potato)") [instant potato](/wiki/Instant_mashed_potatoes "Instant mashed potatoes"), and [Rose's](/wiki/Rose%27s_(marmalade) "Rose's (marmalade)") [marmalade](/wiki/Marmalade "Marmalade"). [Addenbrooke's Hospital](/wiki/Addenbrooke%27s_Hospital "Addenbrooke's Hospital") is a pioneering hospital in the UK, based at [Cambridge Biomedical Campus](/wiki/Cambridge_Biomedical_Campus "Cambridge Biomedical Campus").[[46]](#cite_note-47)

### Universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Universities")]

The most famous university in the region is the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge").[[47]](#cite_note-48) The university has been officially rated as the best in the world in 2010.[[48]](#cite_note-49) It has the second-best medicine course in the world, and in 2010 became the only university outside of the US to raise over £1 billion in charitable donations.

There are eight universities in the region. Cambridge hosts two universities: the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") and [Anglia Ruskin University](/wiki/Anglia_Ruskin_University "Anglia Ruskin University"). It is also the home of the [Open University](/wiki/Open_University "Open University")'s East of England branch. Norwich also hosts two universities: the [University of East Anglia](/wiki/University_of_East_Anglia "University of East Anglia") and [Norwich University of the Arts](/wiki/Norwich_University_of_the_Arts "Norwich University of the Arts"). There are also other towns and cities in the region which have universities including Bedford and Luton ([University of Bedfordshire](/wiki/University_of_Bedfordshire "University of Bedfordshire")), Colchester ([University of Essex](/wiki/University_of_Essex "University of Essex")) and Hatfield ([University of Hertfordshire](/wiki/University_of_Hertfordshire "University of Hertfordshire")). Other higher education centres in the region include [University Centre Peterborough](/wiki/University_Centre_Peterborough "University Centre Peterborough"), [University of Suffolk](/wiki/University_of_Suffolk "University of Suffolk") and [Writtle College](/wiki/Writtle_College "Writtle College").[[49]](#cite_note-Winters_75–88-50)

.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/University_of_Essex.jpg/250px-University_of_Essex.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Essex.jpg)

University of Essex near Colchester

The University of Cambridge receives almost three times as much funding as any other university in the region, due to its huge research grant—the largest in England (and the UK). The next largest, by funding, is [UEA](/wiki/University_of_East_Anglia "University of East Anglia") in Norwich. The [University of Essex](/wiki/University_of_Essex "University of Essex") and [Cranfield University](/wiki/Cranfield_University "Cranfield University") also have moderately large research grants, but no other universities in the region do. The largest university by student numbers is ARU, and the next biggest is Cambridge. The smallest is Essex.[[49]](#cite_note-Winters_75–88-50)

For total income to universities, Cambridge receives around £1 billion—around six times larger than any other university in the region. The [University of Bedfordshire](/wiki/University_of_Bedfordshire "University of Bedfordshire") receives the least income. Cambridge has the lowest drop-out (discontinuation) rate in the region. Once graduated, over 50% of students stay in the region, with 25% going to London and 10% going to the South East. Very few go elsewhere—especially the North of England.[[49]](#cite_note-Winters_75–88-50)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main article: [Sport in England](/wiki/Sport_in_England "Sport in England")

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Football")]

During the nineteenth century, several formulations of the laws of football, known as the [Cambridge rules](/wiki/Cambridge_rules "Cambridge rules"), were created by students at the university. One of these codes, dating from 1863, had a significant influence on the creation of the original [laws](/wiki/Laws_of_the_Game_(association_football) "Laws of the Game (association football)") of [The Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association").[[50]](#cite_note-51)

East of England's top representatives in the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system") today are [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C."), [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C."), [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") and [Luton Town](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C."), who have competed in the top flight at various points. Alongside teams [Peterborough United](/wiki/Peterborough_United_F.C. "Peterborough United F.C."), and [Cambridge United](/wiki/Cambridge_United_F.C. "Cambridge United F.C.").[[51]](#cite_note-52)

### Netball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Netball")]

[London Mavericks](/wiki/London_Mavericks "London Mavericks"), previously Hertfordshire Mavericks and Saracens Mavericks, have competed in the [Netball Super League](/wiki/Netball_Super_League_(United_Kingdom) "Netball Super League (United Kingdom)") since 2005.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-53) The franchise represents the East region and plays a number of home fixtures at the [University of Hertfordshire](/wiki/University_of_Hertfordshire "University of Hertfordshire") sports village and the Brentwood Centre.[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55) Mavericks have appeared in the Netball Super League Grand Final seven times, winning the title in both 2008 and 2011.[[55]](#cite_note-:02-56)

Turnford Netball Club, Norfolk United Netball Club and Hatfield Netball Club are all teams from the East region which play in the [England Netball Premier League](/wiki/England_Netball_Premier_League "England Netball Premier League"), the highest level of club/amateur netball in the country.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Literature")]

Children's author [Dodie Smith](/wiki/Dodie_Smith "Dodie Smith") lived near the town of Sudbury in [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk"), and part of her famous novel [The Hundred and One Dalmatians](/wiki/The_Hundred_and_One_Dalmatians "The Hundred and One Dalmatians") which inspired the [Disney animated film of the same name](/wiki/One_Hundred_and_One_Dalmatians "One Hundred and One Dalmatians") takes place in the town at St Peter's Church.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Media")]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Television")]

Much of the region receives the [BBC East](/wiki/BBC_East "BBC East") and [ITV Anglia](/wiki/ITV_Anglia "ITV Anglia") television services, both based in Norwich (the BBC moving from All Saints' Green to [The Forum](/wiki/The_Forum,_Norwich "The Forum, Norwich") in 2003, and Anglia remaining at its original base, Angia House.) These services broadcast from the [Sandy Heath](/wiki/Sandy_Heath_transmitting_station "Sandy Heath transmitting station"), [Sudbury](/wiki/Sudbury_transmitting_station "Sudbury transmitting station") and [Tacolneston](/wiki/Tacolneston_transmitting_station "Tacolneston transmitting station") transmitter groups. Some areas in close proximity to London, including [Luton](/wiki/Luton "Luton") and south [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), may receive their service from [BBC London](/wiki/BBC_London "BBC London") and [ITV London](/wiki/ITV_London "ITV London"); in addition, the [Hemel Hempstead](/wiki/Hemel_Hempstead "Hemel Hempstead") relay transmitter is a relay of the London services from [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_transmitting_station "Crystal Palace transmitting station"), bringing London television into parts of Hertfordshire. Northwestern parts of [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") including [Kings Lynn](/wiki/Kings_Lynn "Kings Lynn") receive a better TV signal from the [Belmont](/wiki/Belmont_transmitting_station "Belmont transmitting station") transmitter that broadcast [BBC East Yorkshire and Lincolnshire](/wiki/BBC_East_Yorkshire_and_Lincolnshire "BBC East Yorkshire and Lincolnshire") and [ITV Calendar](/wiki/ITV_Calendar "ITV Calendar"). Some editions of *[Look East](/wiki/BBC_Look_East "BBC Look East")* and *[ITV News Anglia](/wiki/ITV_News_Anglia "ITV News Anglia")* broadcast split news programming for the West (Home Counties) and East (East Anglia/Essex) of the region, with the West subregions broadcasting from Sandy Heath; the BBC's Western opt-outs are broadcast from studios in Cambridge, also the base of [BBC Radio Cambridgeshire](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Cambridgeshire "BBC Radio Cambridgeshire"), whilst both versions of the ITV Anglia output have broadcast from Anglia House in Norwich since the split service was introduced in 1990.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=East_of_England&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Radio")]

* [BBC Local Radio](/wiki/BBC_Local_Radio "BBC Local Radio") services in the region include stations for [Cambridgeshire](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Cambridgeshire "BBC Radio Cambridgeshire"), [Essex](/wiki/BBC_Essex "BBC Essex"), [Norfolk](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Norfolk "BBC Radio Norfolk"), [Suffolk](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Suffolk "BBC Radio Suffolk") and [Three Counties Radio](/wiki/BBC_Three_Counties_Radio "BBC Three Counties Radio"), which serves Hertfordshire, Bedfordshire and Buckinghamshire. Radio Cambridgeshire previously broadcast some split programming specific to the Peterborough area - at one point broadcasting this under the BBC Radio Peterborough name - but this opt-out was withdrawn in 2012 as a cost-cutting measure.[[59]](#cite_note-60)
